channel.title: Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG
channel.description: Recent content in Electric Guitar Build 2025-2026 on DAEBFCG

item.title: Pickup Routing
item.description:
The pickups are here!

As planned, these are the quarter pound strat pickups, with optional tap wires. One is a reverse-wind, reverse-polarity (RWRP) so they will be hum-cancelling when used together in series.

The white Seymour Duncan logos are going to be removed. Sorry Seymour.

Now the slots can be routed into the guitar body. This is another example of why it is important to wait until the parts are on hand. These are measuring slightly larger than their online specs.

In search of the perfect 3/4 inch wide hotdog

The goal now is to create a routing template of the ideal proportion. I figure a slot of .75 high by 3.3875 wide will give about 1/32 gap around the edge. This will be made from the 1/4 inch thick acrylic. This took me three tries to get close enough to what I consider acceptable. I started by boring the two end holes with a 3/4 inch forstener bit. Then the challenge is to connect the holes precisely. On the third try, I used the router indexed to the edge of the acrylic sheet to cut the slot edges.

Now for a test route on some scrap.

The fit seems good!

Placement

The locations of the pickup slots are plotted onto the guitar, taking care to ensure they are centered and at the proper angle. The angle is purely for visual consistency with the two string scale lengths. The bridge pickup is 14 degrees, and the neck is 11.21 degrees. These angles are based on the how the frets would lay if extended down this far.

Routing

At this stage, I am not going to route the slots to their final depth. The goal now is to route them just deep enough so that the final depth can be routed later without the template, and the router bit bearing can roll on the existing sides. The reason for this is that once the guitar is cut out there will be no good place to attach the template. But I also want to screw down the template into the waste wood of the pickup slots. So I need to preserve some waste wood in the these slot areas for now. Had to do some thinking to plan this out.

First some starter holes.

Now to route the slots.

Done for now. They are just deep enough that the router bearing can use the existing route as its own template for remainder of the operation.

item.title: Body Assembly
item.description:
First to plug the temporary air holes leading into the hollow chambers.

Some 3/8 plugs of flat sawn walnut are glued in.

Chiseled flush.

Jointing the edges

Now for the task of getting the joints to mate perfectly. This is done almost entirely with the #7 plane. I use a .0025 feeler gauge to check the seam for any gaps.

At one point I tried to fix some high and low spots with a card scraper. This never worked perfectly. Best to trust the plane. When the plane finally takes off a long shaving of the full length of the joint, it is right. Only one or two places and the ends where the thin metal feeler gauge gets through. At some point you have to stop and say it is as good as it will get.

This step was probably the most challenging part of the entire build so far.

Dowels to aid in glueing

I use some short, no longer than 3/8 of an inch, 1/4 inch oak dowels to keep the joints in place while gluing. These are only to keep the pieces from sliding around during the glue up. Not for any structural reinforcement. They are kept very short and the holes as shallow as possible to prevent future stress on the wood.

Both sides just about ready!

Blue side

I glued the blue side first. I was able to use my 8 inch C clamps here, which apply a great deal of pressure and ensure any imperfections in the seam are pressed together flat.

This side joint had some gap issues near the lower end which press together almost completely with the strong C clamps. I stuck some maple plane shavings into the glue of any remaining gap, which should blend right in with the wood invisibly.

The operation was a success!

Red side

Now for the red side. Too wide for any C clamps, so I had to use three bar clamps. Pipe clamps would have been better. Fortunately, this side joint mated very closely and did not required excessive pressure. Although I did tighten the bar clamps as much as possible, to the point the the bars really flexed.

The Result

Overall I am happy with how this turned out. It is starting to look like something.

Some work with the plane again to get the top flat. Curly tiger maple is looking fantastic.

Major milestone reached!

Next up: routing.

Only one more glue joint to make on the entire guitar.

item.title: Fretboard Part 6
item.description:
Now to prepare the fretboard and binding for the glue up.

To being with, I create a layer consisting of masking tape, double sided tape, and craft paper on the bottom of the fretboard. This is so that the binding strips extend down past the bottom edge when being glued down, creating some excess binding on the bottom. This is to try to avoid the binding not coming all the way down to the bottom edge of the fretboard which would be disastrous.

To keep glue out of the slots I wedge some folded Tyvek into each slot, as well as tape off the nut slot.

Each strip is given a final sanding down to 1000 grit and cleaned of any dust.

First the bottom end binding is glued on, made from an off-cut of one of the strips.

Each side piece is glued on, one at a time. The jig is simply a couple strips of particle board screwed together to form a corner with some cork added to the inside edge.

Note use of binding tape to help pull tight, and auxiliary hold-down clamps to keep fretboard flat.

Now all three edges have binding. What looks rough now will soon clean up.

Here is a shot of the bottom showing the excess binding coming proud of the edge as I intended, thanks to the backer layer.

The excess is planed off the top and bottom using a micro plane, and sanded smooth.

Finally, the edges are sanded smooth and square again, taking off the extra 1/32 of an inch and making the bindings their target 1/16 inch thickness. The fretboard is now at the target dimensions, with the exception of the radius across the top. There is also a bit of extra length at the headstock end which I am leaving on for now until final placement on the neck is determined.

Done with the fretboard until final assembly!

item.title: Fretboard Part 5
item.description:
I am going to try adding the side dots to the player-side (blue side) fretboard edge binding before attaching the binding.

For the dots, I am using the curly maple wood used on the rest of the guitar.

Here is the process I have come up with for making wood side dots.

First I cut out ten small blocks and glue them to the ends of dowels with CA gel glue. The grain direction is important and I want the quartersawn side facing out, not the end grain. (Otherwise I would have just used lengthwise sticks.)

I mark the grain direction up side, and roughly sand the blocks to match the cylindrical shape of the dowel.

These are then sharpened to conical points using a pencil sharpener.

Now the 10 points are finely sanded and ready for installation.

The 3/32 diameter holes for the dots are then drilled into a binding strip at the precise locations of the intended fret marker locations.

The vertical position of the line of dots is slightly below center to take into account the resulting fretboard thickness at the edges after being sanded to a 12 inch radius.

Now the dot points are glued into the holes. I do this in two sessions since the 12th fret double-dot markers are too close to be done at the same time. (This explains why I made five double-ended dowels instead of ten.)

The points are then cut off flush and sanded, making some nice looking dots.

Now this binding strip and the matching strip for the other side are ready to be glued to the fretboard.

item.title: Fretboard Part 4
item.description:
For the fretboard edge binding I first select a scrap of walnut that will yield some nice quartersawn strips.

From that I slice off a strip around 3/16 thick and about 3/4 wide. Both binding strips will be made together to keep their thickness equal. The target final size of each strip is 3/8 high by 3/32 thick.

This slice is sanded down close to 3/32 thick.

And then taken down close to the final thickness using a random orbit sander followed by hand sanding.

A few passes with the card scraper and the thickness is right.

Now to cut into two 3/8 inch wide strips using an Exacto knife.

item.title: Fretboard Part 3
item.description:
The fretboard can now be tapered to 1/8 inch narrower than the final width. This is to account for the 1/16 inch edge binding that will be attached to each side.

The taper lines are based on the target overall string-to-string width at the zero fret: 1.44 inches, and the target string width at the bridge: 2.07 inches. From these string lines, I move out 1/16 inch on each side so that once the 1/16 thick bindings are attached there will be 1/8 overhang of fretboard from the outer strings. The width of the fretboard at the zero-fret will end up being about 1.6875 (or 1 and 11/16) which is pretty standard.

Once the cut lines are determined, I tape off the area to keep.

The waste edges are cut off nearly to the tape line on the bandsaw, and then I sneak up to the lines on my disc sander.

Finally, the edges are sanded flat and straight on the soapstone slab (an old antique cabinet top) keeping the edge square.

Ready for binding.

item.title: Blue Side Body Assembly
item.description:
Now to assemble the blue side.

The chambers are first cut out of the center section. I am making the total volume of these chambers less than the red side chamber. The red side control cavity ended up being large and taking out a lot of wood. I want to leave more solid mass around the bridge.

First the back plate is glued down.

After gluing the back plate, I noticed much glue had oozed into the chambers. I was able to cleanly remove it all. But after attaching the top any excess glue that squeezes into the chambers will be impossible to remove. Plus, being mostly air tight, the blobs of glue might never cure and end up molding inside there. So I need to come up with a strategy to prevent this.

My plan is three-fold

  1. Mask off the inside of the top plate where it covers the chamber so that no glue gets applied there, and remove the masking tape just before attaching.

  2. Drill a 3/8 diameter hole from the inside edge of the body into each chamber. This will allow air to get into the chamber and ensure that any glue that does get trapped inside cures properly. These holes will be plugged back up with walnut wood plugs after a week or so of drying time.

  3. Fill the chambers with strips of Tyvek paper that can be pulled out through the aforementioned air holes. These should catch any excess glue that might get squeezed into the chambers.

The air holes drilled:

Filled with Tyvek strips:

The great thing about Tyvek is that it is nearly impossible to tear, so when I pull the strips out I do not have to worry about pieces ripping off and getting stuck inside.

The final glue up:

I pulled out the Tyvek strips after about an hour. Hardly any glue came out with them, so I am hoping the squeeze out into the chambers was minimal.

The assembled side.

First impressions - it is heavy!

item.title: Fretboard Part 2
item.description:
The fret slots can now be cut.

Typically I will mark the locations on one edge and use a machinist square to scribe the lines for the slots at 90 degrees to the center/edge lines. Since this is a multi-scale guitar I need to take a different approach.

I have a template with rows of holes at the fret locations for various scale lengths which I had made by SendCutSend. The template includes rows for 25 and 25.5 which are what I am using here.

I start by marking off lines for the outermost e strings, drawn at a taper from nut to bridge. The string width at the nut is going to be 1.44, and 2.07 at the bridge. Along these string lines, I use the template to mark off the fret locations using the 25.5 for the low e and the 25 for the high e. In my case I can start with the template at the zero-fret since that is the one perpendicular fret on this guitar. On my previous multi-scale guitar I made the 7th fret perpendicular, which causes a more pronounced fan. I think I might prefer the uppermost frets to be less fanned though.

Then it is just a matter of connecting the dots to scribe the fret slot lines.

Once scribed I can then cut the slots. I started off using a machinist square clamped into position for each fret, but soon settled on using my wooden block jig that has sandpaper on the the bottom to keep it from slipping and a magnet embedded on the saw side to help keep the saw against the fence.

Only one slip causing a minor scratch at the 20th fret, which will get sanded out during tapering.

At this stage, I cut the slots quite deep, almost 2/3 of the way through, since the edges will be covered by binding strips. Better to be too deep than not deep enough when installing frets.

The fret slots are now cut, and the all that is left is a notch for the nut. For this guitar, it will be embedded Fender-style into the fretboard. Since this guitar will have a zero-fret the nut mainly serves to keep the strings spaced correctly. I think it looks good placed back about 5/32 (approximate width of a fret and a half) from the zero-fret. Dyed-black bone nut from StewMac.

Now the fret position marker dots can be inlaid down the center line.

I am using dots made from reconstituted turquoise stone, keeping with the native North American theme of this guitar. These should look striking on the walnut board. At first I was a bit disappointed in the look of these as they are rather dull straight out of the package and not as light-green tinted as I had hoped. After test-polishing one, though, the color got quite deeper and looked more pleasing. I believe they will darken up and take on a more reflective sheen once polished and buffed.

Now to just drill the holes with a 1/4 forstener bit on the drill press and CA-glue them in.

item.title: Fretboard Part 1
item.description:
Some unusually humid weather lately so I am taking a break from body-part gluing until things dry out again. A good time to work on the fretboard.

Here is the walnut board I start with. Somewhere in there is a fretboard. The grain is a bit diagonal and I would like the grain lines to be straight up and down so I need to determine the best angle for cutting the edges.

I made a paper negative template than I can lay over the board to best see how the grain lines will look and get a sense of where a good center line will be.

Before trimming down the width I resaw off a 3/8 thick slice to use as the fretboard (the piece of the right). The excess piece on the left will be saved for a future project (ukulele?) so I want to preserve the width.

I decided to use walnut for the fretboard instead of the persimmon I was thinking about. The persimmon is more ideal since it has a higher Janka hardness but the board that I have has some defects on one end and not enough length of good wood for this particular guitar. I will save it for a shorter scale length guitar like the next Super K. My plan is to give the walnut fretboard a good treatment of pure tung oil to seal and harden it up after it is glued to the neck and the frets installed. I like the appearance of the walnut, too, and I think it will be a nice contrast with the lighter maple body wood.

Now I can trim off the sides at the desired angles. Masking tape is used to make the lines fool-proof to see when cutting.

Now for some planing to get the surfaces flat and get the thickness down to the target 1/4 inch.

I stop around 9/32 to give a little bit of extra for final sanding and radiusing.

I like to get the edges trued up too, and use this jointing jig with the #7 plane held upside down in a vise. I got this idea from a Pete Howlett video and it works better than a shooting board for jointing tops and backs and such.

Now the fretboard is dimensioned and ready for the fret slots. At this point the edges are parallel to provide a square reference for the center line. The final taper will be made after cutting the slots.

Some figuring and a couple dark spots have emerged after planing. Ideally that would not have happened but I think I can make it work. The swirly grain figure can go right at the 12th fret, and the dark spot in the middle will land right around the 5th fret. Both these places are significant and will have position-marker inlay dots anyway so hopefully will end up looking more like features rather than flaws. The dark spot close to the edge will probably be mostly removed or at least obscured once the board is tapered.

item.title: Red Side Body Assembly
item.description:
Instead of referring to right and left sides while building a guitar, I have adopted a red/blue terminology to avoid any confusion that can occur when viewing from the back or upside down. Instead of marking L or R, I always just use either a red or blue dot.

This post will be all about putting together the red side.

To prepare for assembly I planed the top and back plates flat. To do this, I screwed them directly to my bench. (I will fill in the holes later.) This photo shows the inside surfaces of the top plates being planed.

The plates are aligned with the inner core using 1/4 inch oak dowels in waste areas. This locks them into place while gluing.

The top plate is glued down. I used a section of 4x4 douglas fir post held in the vise for a clamping jig.

Now that the the top is glued down I can prepare the control cavity. As is, it will be 3/4 inch deep. The top plate is 3/8 thick, which is too thick for the pot and switch posts so will need to be thinned. To thin the top plate I routed out an extra 1/8 inch using a round nose bit to make a nice rounded inside corner. The top plate is now 1/4 inch thick over the control cavity.

Next I sealed the cavity floor and walls with shellac. Then applied copper shielding tape over all the inner surfaces. The shielding tape will form a Faraday cage to prevent noise. It will also be connected to ground, and will automatically ground any components touching it such as metal pot and switch bodies.

Now to cut the access hole in the back plate.

The access hole will be covered by a sheet of 12 gauge brass. The cover plate will be a bit larger all around and will have a shallow recess for it to sit in and provide a surface for it to be screwed down to. To make the recess, I made an acrylic routing template and used a small 1/8 deep flush trim router bit.

As a final touch I rounded over the inside edge of the hole in the back plate, sealed it where it covers the control cavity and applied copped shielding there too. The control cavity is fully shielded!

The back plate was then glued down using the same method as the top plate.

The fully assembled side:

The red side is done. Now for the blue side…
